officeCatalog.name: Sovereign Crown Office
officeCatalog.summary: The ruling crown office of the Maldives during monarchical periods (rendered as Sultan or Queen by holder profile).

functions.description: Absolute sovereign presiding over the Furadaana council and appointing ministerial office-holders.
Sultan ruling through a seven-office cabinet with Kilege as chief minister; Pyrard describes elaborate court ceremony.
Monarch governing through six senior ministers; Christopher describes reduced but still absolute royal authority.
Constitutional monarchy (1932-1953, 1954-1968) with powers progressively curtailed by constitutional reform.

officeCatalog.name: President
officeCatalog.summary: Head of state and government in the republican era.

functions.description: Head of state during the first republic (1953-1954) and transitional period leading to the second republic.
Executive president as head of state and government under the 1968 and 2008 constitutions.

officeCatalog.name: Furadaana (Furadhaana)
officeCatalog.summary: Royal council of Great Officers of State around the sovereign; six Furadaana offices (Faarhanaa, Rannabandeyri, Dhoshimeynaa, Faamuladheyri, Maafaiy, Handeygiri) formed the highest administrative tier below the sovereign and the Uttama Fandiyaaru.

functions.description: Inner council of ministers around the vizier/Kilege; Ibn Battuta describes a structured court hierarchy with distinct judicial and ministerial branches.
Six Great Officers of State (Faarhanaa, Rannabandeyri, Dorhimeyna, Faamuladeyri, Maafaiy, Handeygirin) with defined portfolios; Pyrard describes elaborate ranked seating and ceremony.
Six senior ministerial portfolios under the sovereign; Christopher enumerates specific office-holders with Kilege titles.
Compressed cabinet reported by Bell; fewer distinct offices but concentrated ministerial power among Handeygiri and Faashanaa.
Title persists in social memory; modern cabinet structure replaces traditional Furadaana composition. Office titles abolished by Parliament in 1979.

officeCatalog.name: Kilege
officeCatalog.summary: Highest peerage title bestowed by the sovereign; limited to eight males and three females. Not strictly an office but deeply intertwined with Furadaana offices (e.g., Rannabandeyri Kilegefaanu). Before Islamization (1153 CE), "Ras Kilege" was used as a sovereign style.

functions.description: Pre-Islamic royal epithet "Ras Kilege" transitioned after Islamization to the highest peerage honor; conferred through the gong ceremony.
Head of ministers in the Furadaana cabinet; Kilege titles paired with office names (e.g., Faarhanaa Kilege, Rannabandeyri Kilege). Pyrard documents elaborate investiture protocol.
First-ranked peerage among senior portfolios. Male Kilege titles: Ras, Faarhanaa, Rannabandeyri, Dorhimeyna, Faamuladeyri, Maafaiy, Kaulannaa, Oliginaa, Daharada. Female: Rani, Ma'ava, Kamba'adi.
Three additional Kilege titles created in 1976; all peerage titles abolished by Act of Parliament in 1979.

officeCatalog.name: Faarhanaa
officeCatalog.summary: Premier Furadaana office and one of the three most likely candidates for Prime Minister (Bodu Vazir), alongside Rannabandeyri and Handeygiri.

functions.description: First-ranked Great Officer of State in the Furadaana council; Faarhanaa Kilege was the senior male peerage title after Ras Kilege.
Premier ministerial portfolio; Faarhanaa Kilege holder served as the senior member of the six-minister cabinet.
Continued as one of the Furadaana offices; prime ministerial candidate alongside Rannabandeyri and Handeygiri.

officeCatalog.name: Rannabandeyri
officeCatalog.summary: Furadaana-level Great Officer of State and Keeper of the Crown Jewels; one of the three most likely candidates for Prime Minister. "Ranna" from Sanskrit ratna (jewel). Distinct from the ministerial-level Bodubandeyri and Maabandeyri.

functions.description: Minister of State and Keeper of the Crown Jewels within the Furadaana council; ranked among the six Great Officers.
Continued as Furadaana office with custody of royal regalia; Rannabandeyri Kilege was a senior male peerage title.
One of the premier Furadaana offices; holders could rise to Bodu Vazir (Prime Minister).
Ibrahim Nasir Rannabandeyri Kilegefan served as PM (1957-1968) and signed the independence agreement (1965); became first President of the Second Republic.

officeCatalog.name: Bandeyri
officeCatalog.summary: Generic court/palace portfolio from Sanskrit bhandagara (treasury/storehouse). Parent term for a family of offices: Rannabandeyri (Furadaana-level), Bodubandeyri and Maabandeyri (Vazierun-level). When unqualified, refers to the palace household administration.

functions.description: Palace-household portfolio managing royal residence personnel and court protocol; Pyrard documents it among the principal offices.
Broadened state portfolio encompassing palace administration and selected public functions in Christopher's cabinet description.
Title retained in elite family naming and in the modern Ministry of Finance portal (bandeyri.finance.gov.mv); no formal state function in republican governance.

officeCatalog.name: Bodubandeyri
officeCatalog.summary: Chief Treasurer of the realm, ranking as Vizier (minister-level, below Furadaana). "Bodu" = great/chief in Dhivehi. Managed state treasury, tax collection via atoluverin and kateebs. Distinct from the Furadaana-level Rannabandeyri.

functions.description: Chief Treasurer ranking as Vizier; oversaw fiscal collections including vaaru (poll tax) and varuvaa (land tax) through atoll and island administrators.
Treasury minister in Christopher's cabinet; Bodu Bandeyri Manikufa'anu held the post of Treasurer and Comptroller of Revenue.
Office evolved into the modern Ministry of Finance; the Ministry traces its lineage to the Bodubandeyri in its official history.

officeCatalog.name: Maabandeyri
officeCatalog.summary: Chief of Palace Staff, Keeper of the Royal Seal (Kattiri Mudi), and Minister of the Royal Household. Ranked among the Vazierun (ministers). "Maa" = great/senior in Dhivehi.

functions.description: Minister of the Royal Household; responsible for palace staff, royal residence maintenance, and custody of the royal seal (Kattiri Mudi).
Continued as palace staff chief and royal seal keeper in Christopher's account of the cabinet.

officeCatalog.name: Velaanaa
officeCatalog.summary: Admiral-in-Chief (First Lord of the Admiralty) and Foreign Minister; combined naval and diplomatic portfolio. Ranked among the Vazierun (ministers). Often paired with the Persian-derived title Shahbandar (harbour master).

functions.description: Admiral-in-Chief and Foreign Minister; supervised the Miru Baharu (harbour masters) and managed foreign diplomatic affairs. Pyrard documents the combined naval-diplomatic role.
Continued as the naval and foreign affairs portfolio in Christopher's cabinet; Velaanaa-Shahbandar maintained maritime and diplomatic oversight.
Name survives in the Nasir family house name Velaanaagey; Velana International Airport renamed in 2017 after Ibrahim Nasir's family name.

officeCatalog.name: Faiylia
officeCatalog.summary: Chief Scribe and Keeper of Public Records; managed state documentation, correspondence, and official record-keeping.

functions.description: Chief scribe responsible for royal correspondence and state documentation; also managed receipt and custody of state property in some accounts.
Senior cabinet post managing public records and state correspondence in Christopher's account; office may have encompassed both record-keeping and stores functions.

officeCatalog.name: Dhoshimeynaa
officeCatalog.summary: Commander-in-Chief of Armed Forces; one of the six Furadaana Great Officers of State. Office created by Sultan Mohamed Thakurufaan (r. 1573-1585) who reorganized the military and appointed a Dhoshimeynaa Wazir as head of the security force.

functions.description: Commander-in-Chief and chief military officer in the Furadaana cabinet; Pyrard documents the office as army-general with supreme military authority.
Defense portfolio holder among the six Great Officers; Christopher describes military oversight and command authority.
Ibrahim Dhoshimeynaa Kilegefaanu served as Prime Minister on three occasions between 1883 and 1925, demonstrating the office's political prominence.

officeCatalog.name: Hakuraa
officeCatalog.summary: Minister of Public Works; ranked among the Vazierun (ministers). Evolved from court audience/protocol management to encompass public works infrastructure oversight.

functions.description: Court audience and protocol office; managed formal reception, royal ceremony, and public works.
Minister of Public Works alongside traditional court-protocol duties in Christopher's cabinet description.

officeCatalog.name: Fandiyaaru
officeCatalog.summary: Chief Judge (Uttama Fandiyaaru) with civil and ecclesiastical jurisdiction; ranked ABOVE the Furadaana in precedence, making it the highest office after the sovereign. Responsible for justice, mosque maintenance, charitable trusts (waqf), marriage, and state chronicles.

functions.description: Qadi/chief judge with supreme judicial authority; Ibn Battuta served as Fandiyaaru (1343-1344) and noted the judge's orders were "like the decrees of the King himself, or even stronger." Called "fandiyaaru kaloa."
Grand Pandiare (Pyrard's Gallicization); supreme in ecclesiastical and judicial affairs, independent of the ministerial cabinet. Criminal justice shared with Bandaara Naibu (Attorney General).
Judicial authority maintained alongside the six-minister cabinet; the Uttama Fandiyaaru outranked all Furadaana members in precedence.
Chief judge continuing alongside a compressed ministerial model in Bell's account; consulted with Naibs when deciding cases.
Judicial office transitioning from traditional Qadi to constitutional court system. 1953 Constitution provided for Fandiyaaruge (Court) with Uttama Fandiyaaru and deputies.

officeCatalog.name: Handeygiri
officeCatalog.summary: Administrator and one of the six Furadaana Great Officers of State; one of the three most likely candidates for Prime Minister alongside Faarhanaa and Rannabandeyri.

functions.description: Furadaana-level administrator in the senior cabinet; Handeygirin was among the Great Officers eligible for appointment as Bodu Vazir (Prime Minister).
Powerful ministerial office described by Bell as a central authority figure alongside Faashanaa in the compressed late-monarchy cabinet.
Title survives in elite family naming; historically associated with administrative oversight.

officeCatalog.name: Faamuladheyri
officeCatalog.summary: Minister of State and Royal Treasurer; one of the six Furadaana Great Officers. Before Thakurufaan's military reorganization, Faamuladheyri was one of three offices (with Maafaiy and Daharada) responsible for national security.

functions.description: Minister of State and Royal Treasurer within the Furadaana council; managed state finances alongside the lower-ranked Bodubandeyri (Chief Treasurer).
Senior treasury portfolio among the six Great Officers; Faamuladeyri Kilege holder oversaw royal finances and state budgeting.
Ibrahim Faamuladheyri Kilegefaanu (Ibrahim Ali Didi) appointed first Prime Minister when the Sultanate was restored in 1954.

officeCatalog.name: Maafaiy
officeCatalog.summary: Minister of State; one of the six Furadaana Great Officers. Before Thakurufaan's military reorganization, Maafaiy was one of three offices (with Faamuladheyri and Daharada) responsible for national security.

functions.description: Furadaana-level Minister of State; one of the six Great Officers with broad administrative authority.
Continued as Furadaana office in Christopher's cabinet; Ma'afaiy Kilege was a senior male peerage title.
Ibrahim Maafaiy Thakurufaanu ("Dhon Thuttu") conducted official survey and cartographic work for historic maps of Male in 1921, suggesting a maritime/harbor association.

officeCatalog.name: Faashanaa
officeCatalog.summary: Police and security portfolio; ranked among the Vazierun (ministers). Pre-Islamic predecessor was Fithunaayaka (later Fennaa), the Chief Constable. May be related to the office of Fitna'ayak (Minister for Police).

functions.description: Police and security portfolio reported by Bell as one of the powerful late-monarchy offices alongside Handeygiri.
Office evolved toward the modern police service; Sultan Muhammad Shamsuddeen III formally established a uniformed police force of 120 officers in 1933.

officeCatalog.name: Naib (Naibu / Al-Naib)
officeCatalog.summary: Deputy/representative office from Arabic na'ib. Multiple tiers: Bandaara Naibu (Attorney General) at the central level, Bodu Naibu (province leader) at the atoll level, and island Naibu (Fandiyaaru's local representative) at each inhabited island.

functions.description: Judicial deputies serving as the Fandiyaaru's representatives; Naibs heard complaints (sakuvaa) and implemented religious rulings.
Pyrard documents 13 Naibs in Male implementing laws; Bandaara Naibu served as Attorney General alongside the Fandiyaaru. In the atolls, Naibs served as judicial representatives.
Deputy/viceroy-rank minister in the six-office cabinet described by Christopher; the Naib also functioned as island-level judicial authority.

officeTimeline.label: 14th century (Ibn Battuta era)
officeTimeline.summary: Structured court with a Furadaana council around the vizier, a distinct judicial office (Uttama Fandiyaaru) ranked above all ministers, and Naibs as judicial deputies.

officeTimeline.label: Early 17th century (Pyrard account)
officeTimeline.summary: Full Furadaana council (Faarhanaa, Rannabandeyri, Dhoshimeynaa, Faamuladheyri, Maafaiy, Handeygiri) with Vazierun ministers (Velaanaa, Hakuraa, Bodubandeyri, Maabandeyri) below. Fandiyaaru ranked above all.

officeTimeline.label: 1834 (Christopher account)
officeTimeline.summary: Six-minister cabinet with Furadaana offices (Faarhanaa, Rannabandeyri, Dhoshimeynaa, Faamuladheyri, Maafaiy, Handeygiri) and Vazierun (Velaanaa, Hakuraa, Bodubandeyri, Maabandeyri) documented. Fandiyaaru still distinct.

officeTimeline.label: 1879 (Bell account)
officeTimeline.summary: Compressed cabinet: fewer distinct offices but concentrated power among Handeygiri and Faashanaa alongside the Furadaana. Fandiyaaru continues as chief judge.

officeTimeline.label: 20th century transition
officeTimeline.summary: Constitutional and legal centralization reduced older court-office autonomy. Rannabandeyri and Faamuladheyri Kilege holders served as Prime Ministers. Monarchy ended in 1968; all peerage titles abolished in 1979.

officeTimeline.label: Republican era
officeTimeline.summary: Traditional office titles persist in social memory and elite naming; Bodubandeyri legacy continues via the Ministry of Finance. Formal state leadership is under the presidency.

storyTrails.title: Dhiyamigili -> Huraagey Transition
storyTrails.summary: Walk through late-Dhiyamigili succession dynamics and the long Huraagey consolidation arc into the 20th century.

steps.title: Late Dhiyamigili consolidation
steps.note: Court legitimacy and succession continuity remain centered in the Dhiyamigili line.

steps.title: Ibrahim Iskander period
steps.note: A long reign that stabilizes the late-Dhiyamigili frame before later succession turbulence.

steps.title: Short succession break
steps.note: A brief reign marks the start of rapid handovers in the same political generation.

steps.title: Competing branches
steps.note: Overlapping claims and branch competition become visible in kinship links.

steps.title: Restoration attempt
steps.note: Ghiyathuddine's accession is often treated as a corrective move in succession narratives.

steps.title: Year of rapid transfer
steps.note: Control passes quickly; uncertainty bands widen in some source traditions.

steps.title: Early Huraagey consolidation
steps.note: Muizzuddine anchors the line that later matures into the main late-monarchy dynasty.

steps.title: Institutional stabilization
steps.note: Nooredine's period helps normalize governance after repeated transitions.

steps.title: Long continuity reign
steps.note: Mueenuddine represents a long, comparatively stable sovereign phase.

steps.title: Nineteenth-century extension
steps.note: Imaduddine carries the line into a long 19th-century state-building period.

steps.title: Double accession period
steps.note: Ibrahim Nooredine's two accessions highlight recurring contest and restoration patterns.

steps.title: Late-monarchy high phase
steps.note: Shamsuddine III's long rule is central to final-monarchy constitutional change.

steps.title: Terminal monarchy transition
steps.note: The system enters its final succession phase before republican transformations.

steps.title: Final Sultan
steps.note: Farid's reign closes the monarchy before abolition in 1968.

storyTrails.title: Queens, Claimants, and Succession
storyTrails.summary: Follow major claim transitions around Khadijah and the early Hilaaly-era succession environment.

steps.title: Khadijah's accession cycle
steps.note: Multiple returns to rule make Khadijah a central node for 14th-century legitimacy debates.

steps.title: Intervening claimant
steps.note: Short intervening reign underscores active contest over sovereign office.

steps.title: Another short handover
steps.note: Brief succession windows indicate pressure between rival elite alignments.

steps.title: Raadhaafathi episode
steps.note: A transition point often treated as politically fragile in secondary chronologies.

steps.title: Maakurathu connection
steps.note: The succession merges with linked elite houses in the same period.

steps.title: Post-contest stabilization
steps.note: A longer reign follows repeated turnover, suggesting temporary institutional reset.

steps.title: Two-accession profile
steps.note: Ibrahim's two accessions preserve the pattern of restoration politics.

steps.title: Narrow transition interval
steps.note: Very short-duration sovereign nodes remain critical for accurate lineage mapping.

steps.title: Bridge to later dynastic formations
steps.note: Hadi Hassan sits in the transition corridor preceding clearer later-dynasty structuring.

storyTrails.title: Monarchy to Republic
storyTrails.summary: A compact constitutional narrative from the final sultanate phase to contemporary presidencies.

steps.title: Final monarchy phase
steps.note: Farid's reign defines the final decade of the sovereign crown.

steps.title: Majeed Didi context
steps.note: Prince-level modern reform context intersects with late-monarchy succession politics.

steps.title: First republic interval
steps.note: Amin Didi's presidency marks the first republican constitutional experiment.

steps.title: Second republic foundation
steps.note: Nasir leads the post-monarchy state order after abolition of the sultanate.

steps.title: Long republican consolidation
steps.note: Maumoon's long tenure defines a full-era institutional baseline.

steps.title: Transitional presidency
steps.note: Waheed's period marks a short but important transfer episode.

steps.title: Electoral reset
steps.note: Solih's period represents another democratic cycle realignment.

steps.title: Current presidency
steps.note: Muizzu anchors the present phase in the chronology.

storyTrails.title: Dhiyamigili South Branch
storyTrails.summary: Trace the documented southern Dhiyamigili-linked branch tied to Addu/Fuvahmulah research lines.

steps.title: Source-line sovereign anchor
steps.note: The branch trace starts from late Dhiyamigili sovereign context.

steps.title: Branch divergence context
steps.note: Collateral southern links are usually attached to post-contestation succession phases.

steps.title: Southern collateral bridge
steps.note: Documented as a bridge node in the Dhiyamigili-linked southern genealogy chain.

steps.title: Al-Nabeel Karayye Hassan Didi
steps.note: Another high-salience node in the same southern collateral line.

steps.title: Princess Aishath Didi
steps.note: Represents the downstream continuation of this southern branch in current research coverage.
